title: C++ Array Size
content:
### Determining Array Size

Finding the size of an array is essential for safe iteration and memory management.

---

### Using sizeof

```cpp
int arr[] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};

int totalBytes = sizeof(arr);       // 20 (5 ints * 4 bytes each)
int elementBytes = sizeof(arr[0]);  // 4
int length = sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0]); // 5
```

---

### Modern C++ Methods

**`std::size()` (C++17):**
```cpp
#include <iterator>

int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
size_t len = std::size(arr);  // 5
```

---

### Array Decay Problem

When passed to a function, arrays decay to pointers:

```cpp
void printSize(int arr[]) {
  cout << sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0]); // WRONG: pointer size!
}

int main() {
  int data[10];
  cout << sizeof(data) / sizeof(data[0]); // OK: 10
  printSize(data); // Wrong result (pointer size)
}
```

---

### Solutions

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `std::array` | Fixed-size container (C++11) |
| `std::vector` | Dynamic array |
| Template deduction | `template<size_t N> void func(int (&arr)[N])` |

---

> **Key Point:** `sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0])` only works in the scope where the array was declared. After decay to a pointer, this method fails. Use `std::size()` or `std::array` instead.

quiz.question: What happens when an array is passed to a function?
